title: Spain
subtitle: 1900–1975 · Crisis, Republic, Civil War and Dictatorship

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: Monarchy, Republic, military coup and dictatorship

entries.label: Alfonso XIII — the last Restoration king
entries.hint: Alfonso XIII reigns through colonial collapse, the 1923 military coup, and growing republican pressure — he goes into exile on 14 April 1931 after republican parties win local elections, interpreted as a referendum against the monarchy.
entries.note: Alfonso XIII's abdication letter (14 Apr 1931): published in full in La Gaceta de Madrid (official gazette) and major newspapers of the day. The 1931 local election results — the trigger for his departure — are in the Archivo Histórico Nacional (AHN). Preston (2004), Juan Carlos: A People's King, ch.1 for context.

entries.label: Second Spanish Republic proclaimed
entries.hint: The Second Republic is proclaimed from the balcony of the Interior Ministry at the Puerta del Sol — a jubilant popular moment; the Republic will govern through four phases before the Civil War destroys it.
entries.note: Proclamation 14 April 1931: La Gaceta de Madrid, 15 Apr 1931 — the official gazette records the Provisional Government. Photographs: Agencia EFE archive. The Provisional Government's composition: documented in the same gazette. Carr (1982), Spain 1808–1975: the standard English-language history of the Republic.

entries.label: Franco's coup and the military uprising
entries.hint: The military uprising begins in Spanish Morocco on 17 July 1936 — within hours Franco broadcasts his manifesto; the coup fails to take Madrid and Barcelona immediately, triggering three years of civil war rather than a quick seizure of power.
entries.note: Franco's manifesto (18 Jul 1936): text published in Diario de Burgos and preserved in AHN. The telegram from Melilla beginning the uprising: documented in military archives. Thomas (1961), The Spanish Civil War: the foundational English-language study. Jackson (1965), The Spanish Republic and the Civil War.

entries.label: Franco declared Generalísimo and head of state
entries.hint: The Nationalist junta elevates Franco to supreme command and head of government — the decree is published in the official Nationalist bulletin; his consolidation of personal power over rival generals begins immediately.
entries.note: Boletín Oficial de la Junta de Defensa Nacional, 1 Oct 1936: the decree text. The internal politics of the elevation: Preston (1993), Franco: A Biography, ch.10. The elimination of rival Nationalist leaders (Mola, José Antonio Primo de Rivera) consolidates Franco's position through 1937.

entries.label: Franco — 36 years of dictatorship
entries.hint: Francisco Franco rules Spain from April 1939 to his death on 20 November 1975 — the longest dictatorship in 20th-century Western Europe; his regime archives are partly in the AHN and partly still classified.
entries.note: Franco's death certificate 20 Nov 1975: registered in Madrid civil registry. The Valle de los Caídos decree (Apr 1940): BOE. Franco's 36 years of rule: documented in Boletín Oficial del Estado (BOE), the official state gazette, throughout. Preston (1993), Franco: A Biography: the standard modern biography (900pp).

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Civil war, international intervention and Francoist repression

entries.label: Military uprising begins — 17 July 1936
entries.hint: The uprising starts in Spanish Morocco — Franco flies from the Canary Islands in a chartered British plane to take command; within 48 hours Spain is divided between Republican government and Nationalist rebels.
entries.note: Franco's flight in the Dragon Rapide: documented in the plane's manifest (preserved). The uprising's spread across garrisons: documented in military and newspaper archives from both sides. Thomas (1961), The Spanish Civil War, Part I. The Dragon Rapide charter: Rankin (2003), Telegram from Guernica.

entries.label: Bombing of Guernica — 26 April 1937
entries.hint: The Condor Legion and Italian Air Force bomb the Basque market town of Guernica for three hours on a busy market day — the number of dead is disputed (126–1,654 in sources) but the symbolic impact is immediate and global; Picasso begins his painting within days.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Wolfram von Richthofen diary entries, 18 and 23 April 1937: preserved in German military archives (Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv) — describe the operation planning. Eyewitness: Father Alberto Onaindía (Basque priest, present that day) — his testimony published 1937. George Steer, The Times of London, 28 Apr 1937 (journalist, arrived hours after bombing). Casualty dispute: Basque government reported 1,654; local historians revised to 126–153. The uncertainty must be flagged.

entries.label: Battle of the Ebro — final Republican offensive
entries.hint: The largest battle of the Civil War — the Republic crosses the Ebro with 80,000 troops in a desperate effort to relieve Valencia; initial success is reversed over four months; the Republic loses 70,000 casualties it cannot replace.
entries.note: Republican military records (fragmentary): Archivo General Militar de Ávila. International Brigades records: ALBA digital archive (UCSD). Thomas (1961) ch.55–59. The battle as the Republic's last major offensive: Graham (2002), The Spanish Republic at War. International press coverage: New York Times archive, Le Monde archive.

entries.label: Non-Intervention and the International Brigades
entries.loc: Madrid; Jarama; Teruel (main IB engagement sites)
entries.hint: The Non-Intervention Agreement (27 powers) allows Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy to support Franco while nominally maintaining neutrality — the International Brigades (35,000 volunteers from 50 countries) fight for the Republic; their records survive in the ALBA archive.
entries.note: Non-Intervention Agreement 1936: text in League of Nations archives, Geneva. ALBA (Abraham Lincoln Brigade Archives): digital collection at UCSD — 150+ letters, photographs, personnel records. British Trades Union Congress archive: 46 files on the Civil War (IHR, London). Orwell, Homage to Catalonia (1938): eyewitness by a participant — first edition British Library.

entries.label: White Terror — Francoist mass killings 1936–1945
entries.loc: Spain-wide; mass graves throughout Andalusia, Extremadura, Castile
entries.hint: Francoist forces systematically execute Republican soldiers, officials and civilians during and after the Civil War — the Historical Memory Law (2007) initiated a mass graves programme; current estimates range from 100,000 to 200,000 killed in political repression.
entries.note: UNCERTAIN: Casualty figures disputed. Centro Documental de la Memoria Histórica (Salamanca): the primary archive. Mass graves database: Ministerio de la Presidencia, online from 2007. Preston (2012), The Spanish Holocaust: the most comprehensive modern study, with detailed regional evidence. The 'pact of silence' during the Transition limited documentation; ongoing excavations continue to revise numbers.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Constitutions, laws and the architecture of the Francoist state

entries.label: Constitution of the Second Republic — 1931
entries.hint: The 1931 Constitution separates Church and State, grants women the vote (the first time in Spanish history), establishes regional autonomy, and secularises education — it is the most progressive Spanish constitution to that date.
entries.note: Text: Gaceta de Madrid, 10 Dec 1931 (extant, digitised). Women's suffrage article 36: confirmed by vote in Cortes (debated by Clara Campoamor vs Victoria Kent — both women deputies). The Cortes debates on women's suffrage: Diario de Sesiones de las Cortes Constituyentes 1931. Arranz (2007), La Constitución de 1931.

entries.label: Fuero del Trabajo — Francoist labour charter
entries.hint: Franco's labour charter — the first of the Leyes Fundamentales that together constitute the 'organic' Francoist state; it abolishes independent trade unions, bans strikes, and creates a corporatist 'vertical syndicate' system.
entries.note: Boletín Oficial del Estado (BOE), 10 Mar 1938: text of the Fuero del Trabajo. The six Leyes Fundamentales together: AHN documentation. Payne (1987), The Franco Regime 1936–1975: the standard academic analysis of the legal structure.

entries.label: Ley de Responsabilidades Políticas — retrospective repression
entries.hint: The Political Responsibilities Law holds all who 'opposed the National Movement' from October 1934 onwards legally liable — giving the regime a legal framework to confiscate property and imprison Republicans retroactively going back five years.
entries.note: BOE 13 Feb 1939: text of the law. The retroactivity to 1934 was historically unprecedented in Spanish law. Preston (1993), Franco, ch.20 on the repression's legal architecture. The law created 40,000+ proceedings: AHN Fondos del Tribunal Nacional de Responsabilidades Políticas.

entries.label: Statute of Autonomy of Catalonia — the Estatut
entries.hint: The first Statute of Catalan Autonomy, approved by referendum (592,961 votes to 3,276) and ratified by the Madrid Cortes — giving Catalonia its own government (Generalitat) and official status for the Catalan language; suppressed by Franco in 1939.
entries.note: Gaceta de Madrid, 21 Sep 1932: text of the Estatut. The referendum result: documented in the Archivo del Parlament de Catalunya. The Generalitat archives (ANC, Barcelona): primary repository. The Estatut's suppression by Franco: documented in BOE 1938–39. The 1932 statute became the template for all subsequent Catalan autonomy statutes.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: National Catholicism, anticlerical violence and the Church-State alliance

entries.label: National Catholicism — Franco's ideological fusion
entries.hint: Franco fuses Catholic identity with Spanish nationalism — the Church receives extraordinary privileges (censorship power, education control, tax exemption) in exchange for legitimating the regime; the Concordat of 1953 formalises this arrangement.
entries.note: Concordat of 1953 (Spain-Vatican): text in BOE 19 Oct 1953. The National Catholicism ideology: analysed in Lannon (1987), Privilege, Persecution and Prophecy: The Catholic Church in Spain. The Church's educational role: documented in Education Ministry records (AHN). Pérez-Díaz (1993), The Return of Civil Society on the Church's post-Franco evolution.

entries.label: Anticlerical violence — burning of churches 1931 and 1936
entries.hint: Churches and convents are burned in May 1931 (Madrid, Málaga) by Republican crowds and more extensively in July–September 1936 at Civil War's outbreak — approximately 7,000 clergy killed in Republican territory; documented in Church martyrology.
entries.note: The 1936 killings: documented in Causa General (1943) — a Francoist legal inquiry, biased but containing real data. Modern reassessment: Montero Moreno (1961), Historia de la persecución religiosa en España 1936–1939. The 7,000 figure: Callahan (2000), The Catholic Church in Spain. FLAG: the Causa General data requires critical handling as a Francoist propaganda document as well as a historical source.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Guernica, the Generation of '98, Lorca and Republican culture

entries.label: Guernica — Picasso's response to the bombing
entries.hint: Picasso paints Guernica in six weeks after reading George Steer's newspaper report — 3.49m × 7.76m, oils on canvas; it becomes the defining image of 20th-century political art and the most discussed painting of the Spanish Civil War.
entries.note: Extant: Museo Reina Sofía, Madrid (since 1992, transferred from MoMA). Picasso began painting May 1937. The preparatory sketches (45 studies): also Reina Sofía. Van Hensbergen (2004), Guernica: The Biography of a Twentieth-Century Icon. The painting's political history — kept in MoMA throughout the Franco years at Picasso's explicit request — is itself a primary source on Spain-US relations.

entries.label: Generation of '98 — crisis of Spanish identity
entries.hint: The intellectual response to the 1898 colonial disaster produces a generation of writers who interrogate Spanish identity — Unamuno, Azorín, Baroja, Machado; their essays and novels are the primary evidence for elite Spanish self-questioning at the century's opening.
entries.note: Unamuno, En torno al casticismo (1902): first edition, Biblioteca Nacional de España (BNE). Antonio Machado, Campos de Castilla (1912): BNE first edition. The Residencia de Estudiantes (Madrid, 1910–1936): archive held at the Residencia itself — documents the intellectual network connecting the '98 generation to the Republic. Fox (1997), Reimagining Spain: the standard English study.

entries.label: Federico García Lorca — poet, playwright, martyr
entries.hint: Lorca is shot by Nationalist forces in August 1936 near Granada — his body has never been found; his Romancero Gitano (1928) and Poeta en Nueva York (written 1929–30) are the most celebrated Spanish poems of the 20th century; his death became the defining symbol of Francoist cultural repression.
entries.note: Lorca shot 18 Aug 1936: documented in multiple contemporary accounts; Gibson (1983), Federico García Lorca: A Life, the standard biography. The location of his burial: subject of ongoing forensic investigation (mass grave near Víznar, Granada) — as of 2024 not conclusively located. Romancero Gitano first edition 1928: BNE. Poet in New York first edition 1940 (posthumous): BNE.

entries.label: Valle de los Caídos — monument built by political prisoners
entries.loc: San Lorenzo de El Escorial, Madrid (extant)
entries.hint: Franco orders a vast underground basilica cut into a granite mountain using Republican political prisoners as forced labour — 558m tunnel, 150m cross visible for 30km; Franco himself is buried here until 2019 when his remains are exhumed and moved.
entries.note: Construction decree: BOE Apr 1940. The use of forced labour: documented in Sueiro (1976), El Valle de los Caídos. Franco's burial: documented 1975; exhumation 24 Oct 2019: BOE, Supreme Court ruling. Extant (closed since 2019, transformation ongoing). The monument is itself a primary source for Francoist ideology of 'reconciliation' through Catholic ritual.

entries.label: Flamenco and Republican culture — Carmen Amaya
entries.hint: Carmen Amaya, born in a shack by the Barcelona waterfront, becomes the greatest flamenco dancer of the 20th century — her 1936 performance tour is interrupted by the Civil War, she goes into exile in South America, returns under Franco; her career documents the survival of Romani-Andalusian culture across the rupture.
entries.note: Carmen Amaya's career: documented in contemporary press and film. Her 1963 film Amor de Dios (extant). Pohren (1964), The Art of Flamenco: primary documentation of her technique. The Romani community's experience under Francoism: Castro Varela & Dhawan (2020). Her passport and emigration records: Archivo General de la Administración (AGA), Alcalá de Henares.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Intellectuals, exile and the suppression of free thought

entries.label: Ortega y Gasset — The Revolt of the Masses (1930)
entries.hint: José Ortega y Gasset's La Rebelión de las Masas is the most internationally influential Spanish philosophical text of the 20th century — a diagnosis of European mass society that sells 15,000 copies in its first year and is translated into 20 languages.
entries.note: First edition: Revista de Occidente, Madrid 1930: BNE. The Revista de Occidente (1923–1936) itself: key intellectual journal of the era, archive at BNE. Moran (1996), El maestro en el erial: Ortega y Gasset and the Second Republic. The book's international reception: documented in translation records and press reviews.

entries.label: George Orwell — Homage to Catalonia (1938)
entries.hint: Orwell fights with the POUM militia on the Aragon front, witnesses the Communist-Anarchist street fighting in Barcelona (May 1937), is shot through the throat, and barely escapes — his eyewitness account is the most vivid English-language primary source for the Republic's internal conflicts.
entries.note: First edition: Secker & Warburg, London 1938 — British Library holds the first edition. Orwell's POUM militia card (extant): documents his service. His medical records from the bullet wound: documented in Orwell's own correspondence (University College London, Orwell Archive). Crick (1980), George Orwell: A Life. The book sold only 638 copies in Orwell's lifetime.

entries.label: Republican propaganda posters — a visual primary corpus
entries.hint: The Spanish Republic produces over 2,000 propaganda posters — lithographs by Renau, El Lissitzky-influenced designs by various artists — constituting the richest single corpus of political graphic design of the 20th century, now digitised by multiple archives.
entries.note: Brandeis University Spanish Civil War Collection: 5,000+ items. BNE digital collection: hundreds of posters. Cary (1973) on Republican poster art. The Nationalist counter-poster tradition: smaller and less artistically significant. Southworth Collection (UCSD): the most comprehensive private collection, now digitised. The posters as primary evidence for Republican ideology: Brihuega (1982).

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Economy from colonial collapse to autarky to technocratic growth

entries.label: Disaster of 1898 — colonial collapse and economic crisis
entries.hint: Spain loses Cuba, Puerto Rico and the Philippines to the United States in the Treaty of Paris (December 1898) — the loss of the last colonial markets triggers a crisis of national identity and economic restructuring that sets the century's political agenda.
entries.note: Treaty of Paris, 10 Dec 1898: US Senate ratification records; Spanish text in Gaceta de Madrid. The economic impact: Tortella (2000), The Development of Modern Spain, ch.5. The repatriation of 200,000 soldiers from Cuba: documented in military records (AHN). The indemnity paid to the US ($20m): documented in Treasury records.

entries.label: Francoist autarky — the hungry decade
entries.hint: Franco's autarky policy (economic self-sufficiency) produces a decade of stagnation and hunger — rationing cards are in use until 1952; Spain's GDP does not recover to 1935 levels until 1951; the black market (estraperlo) becomes pervasive.
entries.note: Rationing records: AGA, Alcalá de Henares. Ration cards (extant objects): multiple museum collections. GDP data: Prados de la Escosura (2003), El progreso económico de España. The estraperlo (black market): documented in police and court records. Viñas et al. (1979), Política comercial exterior en España 1931–1975: the standard economic history.

entries.label: Opus Dei technocrats and the Plan de Estabilización (1959)
entries.hint: Franco appoints Opus Dei-linked technocrats who impose austerity and open Spain to foreign investment — the 1959 Stabilisation Plan ends autarky and triggers the 'Spanish miracle' of 6% annual growth in the 1960s.
entries.note: Plan de Estabilización decree: BOE, Jul 1959. World Bank and IMF involvement: documented in their archives (Washington DC). Payne (1987), The Franco Regime, ch.17 on the technocrats. GDP growth 1960–1973: Prados de la Escosura (2003). The plan's social consequences — mass internal migration from Andalusia and Extremadura to Catalonia and Basque Country — documented in census records.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: International intervention, exile and Cold War diplomacy

entries.label: Non-Intervention Agreement — European powers abandon the Republic
entries.hint: 27 European states sign the Non-Intervention Agreement while Germany and Italy openly supply Franco — the most cynical diplomatic exercise of the interwar period; the Republic receives Soviet aid but at a political cost (Soviet interference in Republican politics).
entries.note: Non-Intervention Committee records: National Archives (UK), FO 849 series. German military aid (Operation Magic Fire): Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv. Italian aid: ACS (Rome). Soviet aid: RGVA (Moscow), partly published in Radosh et al. (2001), Spain Betrayed. Thomas (1961) ch.25–28 synthesises the international dimension.

entries.label: Republican exile — 500,000 flee to France (1939)
entries.loc: Pyrenean border crossings; Argelès-sur-Mer (French internment camps)
entries.hint: Half a million Republicans flee to France in the Retirada (February–March 1939) — the largest refugee movement in European history to that date; they are interned in camps on the beaches; many go on to Mexico, which receives the Republican government in exile.
entries.note: French government records: Archives nationales (Paris), series F7 (refugees). The Retirada's scale: documented in French military and police records. Mexican reception: Archivo Histórico Diplomático Mexicano. Pons Prades (1977), Republicanos españoles en la Segunda Guerra Mundial. Martínez (2007) on the Republican exile community.

entries.label: US-Spain Pacts of Madrid (1953) — Cold War legitimacy
entries.hint: The United States signs the Pacts of Madrid with Franco — giving the US military bases in Spain in exchange for economic aid and diplomatic recognition; the pacts legitimise the dictatorship internationally and mark Spain's integration into Cold War Western structures.
entries.note: Pacts of Madrid (Mutual Defence Assistance Agreement, 26 Sep 1953): US National Archives (NARA), State Department records. Declassified US diplomatic cables on the negotiation: FRUS 1952–54 Vol.VI. Viñas (2003), En las garras del águila: the standard Spanish study. The simultaneous Concordat with the Vatican (July 1953): the two agreements together define the Francoist international settlement.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Spain in its 20th-century European and global context

entries.label: Spain's neutrality in World War II
entries.hint: Franco maintains nominal neutrality while sending the Blue Division (47,000 volunteers) to fight alongside Germany on the Eastern Front — the Blue Division allows Franco to maintain the German alliance without formal belligerency, documented in German and Spanish military records.
entries.note: Blue Division records: Archivo General Militar de Ávila; German Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv. The 47,000 volunteers: documented in enlistment records. Kleinfeld & Tambs (1979), Hitler's Spanish Legion: the standard study. The neutrality negotiations: US NARA, State Department decimal files on Spain. Franco's Hendaye meeting with Hitler (23 Oct 1940): German Foreign Ministry records (PAAA, Berlin).

entries.label: UN ostracism and rehabilitation of Franco's Spain
entries.hint: The 1946 UN resolution recommends member states withdraw ambassadors from Spain (45 to 2 vote) — Spain is isolated; by 1955 the Cold War reverses this and Spain is admitted to the UN; the complete diplomatic rehabilitation of a Fascist regime by Western powers.
entries.note: UN Resolution 39(I), 12 Dec 1946: UN General Assembly records (digitised). The ambassador withdrawals: documented in diplomatic correspondence (AHN). Spain's UN admission 14 Dec 1955: UN records. The US role in rehabilitation: FRUS 1952–54 Vol.VI. Payne (1987), The Franco Regime, ch.16 on the Cold War transformation.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored · including categorical shifts

entries.label: Clara Campoamor — architect of women's suffrage
entries.hint: Clara Campoamor argues for women's suffrage in the 1931 Constituent Cortes against opposition from fellow Republican Victoria Kent — her speech on 1 October 1931 is the primary document of Spanish feminism; women vote for the first time in the November 1933 elections.
entries.note: Campoamor's speech, 1 Oct 1931: Diario de Sesiones de las Cortes Constituyentes, 1 Oct 1931 (digitised, Congreso de los Diputados archive). The vote: Article 36 of the 1931 Constitution. Campoamor (1936), El voto femenino y yo: her own memoir of the suffrage campaign, BNE. Nash (1991), Defying Male Civilization: Women in the Spanish Civil War.

entries.label: Ley de Vagos y Maleantes — criminalisation of homosexuality
entries.hint: The 1933 Vagrancy Law is amended in 1954 to explicitly include homosexuals as subject to detention and forced labour camps — a CATEGORICAL SHIFT from implicit social stigma to explicit criminal category; replaced by the even harsher Ley de Peligrosidad Social (1970).
entries.note: BOE 17 Jul 1954: the amendment text. The Ley de Peligrosidad y Rehabilitación Social (1970): BOE 6 Aug 1970 — replaced the 1954 law with even more explicit criminalisation. Ugarte Pérez (2008), Una discriminación universal: la homosexualidad bajo el franquismo: the primary modern study. The internment camps (especially Badajoz): documented in prison records, partly in AGA. The transition to democracy formally decriminalised homosexuality in 1979.

entries.label: Mujeres Libres — anarchist women's organisation 1936–1939
entries.hint: Mujeres Libres (Free Women) organises 20,000 members during the Civil War — running schools, childcare centres and training programmes; their magazine and archive is the primary source for women's autonomous political organising in the Spanish Republic.
entries.note: Mujeres Libres magazine (1936–1938): digitised, BNE and IISG Amsterdam. The organisation's records: International Institute of Social History (IISG), Amsterdam — the primary archive. Ackelsberg (1991), Free Women of Spain: Anarchism and the Struggle for the Emancipation of Women: the standard scholarly study. Membership figure of 20,000: documented in the organisation's own publications.

entries.label: Forced labour under Francoism — Republican prisoners
entries.loc: Valle de los Caídos; Cuelgamuros; railway and canal construction sites
entries.hint: Franco uses Republican political prisoners as forced labour on construction projects including the Valle de los Caídos — an estimated 20,000 prisoners work at the site; the legal mechanism (Redención de Penas por el Trabajo) frames exploitation as 'redemption'.
entries.note: Redención de Penas por el Trabajo decree (1939): BOE. The Valle de los Caídos forced labour: documented in Sueiro (1976) and Aguilar (2008), Memory and Amnesia. Prisoner records: AHN. The number 20,000 at Valle de los Caídos: documented in Fernández-Poyatos (1995). This is a CATEGORICAL SHIFT: forced labour reframed as spiritual 'redemption' — one of the most cynical legislative manoeuvres of the dictatorship.

events.l: Forced labour — Valle de los Caídos
